utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: patient's name is diana scott . date of birth , 12/8/1920 . the date of service is 7/9/2021 . this is a new patient note .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good afternoon . how are you today ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm good , thank you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good . well , what brings you in to see me today ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well , my doctor says that i have a heart murmur .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so i do n't know how big a heart murmur i have , or really even what it is .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . so how long have you had that for ? do you have any idea , or is that completely new as far as you know ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , she said it's a new worrisome heart murmur . so of course , i'm worried as well .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: got it . okay . and are you having any symptoms at all ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do n't think so . i do n't know what symptoms i should be having .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . well , so you are 100 , so we ca n't really get around that one . so i expect that you probably do n't move quite as fast as maybe you used to ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , i definitely do n't .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and do you notice that you have any chest pain or f- feel shortness of breath ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , i do n't have any chest pain . um , my problem is i have severe pains in my legs , and so she had me going for a chest x-ray , and that was just last week .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: and so i started taking pain pills for my legs .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , got it , for the legs . and do you feel short of breath at all ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: if i walk too fast or too long , or if i tried to drink a whole glass of water without stopping , then yeah , i do get short of breath that way .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and how far can you walk before you feel short of breath or sort of overly fatigued ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well , i do have a walker now , so i do n't know . maybe when i walk to the end of the driveway or i walk around the culdesac .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: got it . all right .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so it is n't that bothersome to me at all . um , maybe more if i try to overdo it .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: then i , um , i get short of breath if i do overdo it though .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , got it . and so how different would you say , um , that the symptoms are now compared to like six months ago or a year ago ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i would say probably 70 to 80 % from about six months ago . before that i did n't notice anything at all .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . so do you feel like it's mainly the leg pain that seems to limit you ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: my legs have been severe for about a month now .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and how about , do you feel lightheaded or dizzy at all ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah . so i do take a pill for that when it does get bad .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , and i do n't take it every day , only when i do feel dizzy .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and so how about any passing out at all ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , good . and how about irregular heartbeats ? do you ever feel like your heart is going too fast or like it skips a beat ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do n't notice it at all .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , great . and how about any swelling in the legs at all ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , my ankles swell .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and how long has this been going on ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , i would say for probably six months . but it goes up and down , and sometimes it's worse than other times .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and how about any recent weight gain or anything else like that ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , i lost a little bit of weight , but i still weigh a 120 pounds , but i normally weigh about 130 pounds .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm . okay .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , but that's been going down gradually for about a year .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and then how about any fevers , chills or anything else that's , that's going on that you can think of ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good . so mainly it sounds like you came in , um , mostly because of the murmur . is that right ? and sort of just kind of seeing what things look like ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , that and i feel overly tired because i take all that medication .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and so fatigue as well . and have you ever had any testing of your heart done ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm not really sure .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . it does look like you've had a decent number of sh- , uh , surgeries here , ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , you name it .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right . well , i wo n't make you repeat them since i do have a good record . um , but just give me just a second and i'm going to enter the ones we need , okay ? all right . got them . and then , are you taking all the medicines that you went through with madison ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , good . and looking at your family history , it looks like maybe your brother had a heart attack . is that correct ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it was actually my son , not my brother .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , i'll make that adjustment .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , but my brother did have a stroke . but that was because he had gotten sugar diabetes , and my eldest sister did have a heart problem as well .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , i see .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , so heart problems kinda run in the family .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . all right . well , it looks like also that you never smoked . that's excellent . and then no allergies to medicines that you know of , is that correct ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: correct .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right . well , let's do a quick exam .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: all right . sounds good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right . well , for physical exam , please use the regular template . all right , please just breathe normally . great . and now take a few deep breaths for me . all right . do you feel okay ? are you feeling any dizziness or anything ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , i feel fine .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , great . all right . well , that is it for the exam .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right . so what i'm thinking is , let's go ahead and schedule you for an echocardiogram . it's a very common test and it just takes a picture of your heart . and it'll let me see how well your heart is actually working .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and i can order that for today . um , do you have time to complete that ? it should n't take too long .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes , that's fine . i have time .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , great . so we will complete that today , um , to assess your overall cardiac structure and function , as well as , uh , the valve view of where diseased .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and some hospital outcomes and what we might suggest to fix that out , i'll go over that . so one possible suspicion i'm having is it's called , um , aortic stenosis , and that just means that one of your main valves of your heart has narrowed a bit and the valve does n't really open , um , quite fully . and this could just reduce or block the blood flow from your heart into the main artery of your body , and that's called the aorta , into the rest of your body .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and if this is severe , then we might wan na perform a procedure called the transcatheter aortic valve replacement , or we just call it tavr . and it is a minimally invasive procedure to replace the valve .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well , i would have to think about that . i'm not too sure i'd wan na do that , given my age of all .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah , sure , no problem . and we do n't have to make any decisions today , just , you know , wanted to go over a couple of these things . um , but sometimes we can also perform a surgical aortic valve replacement , or it's called an savr . but really , because of your age and medical history , um , unfortunately , that would n't be a good option for you .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i see . okay . well , i guess we'll see , um , what you see from , from the , um , test .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah , agreed . okay . and , um , so couple other things real quick . so it looked like your blood pressure looked really good today . so we're not going to change your amlodipine or lisinopril . so just continue taking those as you are currently .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay , good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and then , it also looks like you're taking about 20 milligrams of simvastatin for your lipids . and i will pr- , um , defer to your pcp . however , given you are having leg pain in both your legs , and given your age and no history of coronary artery d- , artery disease , it is possible that you might be able to stop your statin .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: really ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah , um , possibly . he would have to evaluate you , but it is certainly something to discuss with him . so i will send over the results of the echocardiogram and also my recommendations and then you guys can talk about the statin .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay , that sounds great . thank you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you're welcome . and then , um , lastly , your hyperthyroidism , uh , i see you're taking levothyroxine . and again , no change here and you can just keep taking that as you have them .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay , i will .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right . so we will see you back here in about three or four weeks to discuss the results and your next steps . um , and we'll , uh , take care of your echocardiogram today . um , any questions ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: not that i can think of . thank you so much . and i think it looks good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , awesome . all right . well , have a good rest of your day , and we'll see you soon .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: for physical exam , constitutional elderly otherwise no acute distress . cardiovascular normal s1 and s2 is preserved with a normal rate and regular rhythm . there is a 4/6 systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border with , uhm , mild radiation to the carotids . neurologic gait is normal for age .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: for assessment and plan , probably one cardiac murmur . diana has a 4/6 systolic murmur at the upper right sternal border with mild radiation to the carotids . this is most likely representative of aortic stenosis , for symptoms could be due to severe aortic , aortic stenosis . however , her s2 is relatively preserved , which would be more consistent with , consistent with moderate . some of her outside records do note diastolic dysfunction , so it is possible she , she did have an echocardiogram at some point in the past . she does not appear significantly volume overloaded today .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: her next problem is pvcs . her referral mentioned tachycardia . uh , her ekg today shows sinus rhythm with frequent pvcs , as well as an anterior septum infarct pattern . she does not have any significant palpitations and we will check an echocardiogram to assess overa- overall cardiac structure and function . depending on results as well as the severity of her aortic stenosis , we will need to consider an ischemic evaluation , or further , possibly a heart mon- monitor to assess overall burden of the pvcs . at this point , she's asymptomatic , so we will start with the echocardiogram only .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: her next problem , hypolipidemia , is managed by her pcp . due to bilateral leg pain over the last month , considered tavr evaluation following echocardiogram . statin could be indicated .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and next problem , lightheadedness , dizziness . she is on meclizine for this . it is possible that some of her lightheadedness and dizziness is related to the aorta stenosis , and we will do an echocardiogram as noted above . pvcs on her ekg could be contributing , but she does not feel palpitations . we will consider a monitor , but start with the echocardiogram first as noted above . follow up three to four weeks after her echocardiogram is done to discuss results and next steps . end of dictation .

detail_list.summary: The patient is a 100-year-old female named Diana Scott.
Heart murmur
The doctor asks about the duration of the heart murmur.
The patient reports the heart murmur is new and described as worrisome.
The doctor asks if the patient is experiencing any symptoms.
The doctor asks if the patient has chest pain or shortness of breath.
The patient denies chest pain and reports severe bilateral leg pain for one month, for which she had a chest x-ray last week.
The patient reports shortness of breath with exertion or drinking water quickly.
The patient uses a walker and can walk around the cul-de-sac or to the end of the driveway without significant shortness of breath.
The patient reports a 70-80% decline in functioning over the last six months.
The doctor asks if the patient feels lightheaded or dizzy.
The patient reports occasional dizziness and takes medication as needed.
The doctor asks if the patient has experienced any syncope.
The doctor asks if the patient experiences palpitations or irregular heartbeats.
The doctor asks if the patient has leg swelling.
The patient reports ankle swelling for the past six months.
The doctor asks about recent weight changes.
The patient reports a weight decrease from 130 to 120 pounds over the last year.
The doctor asks about fevers or chills.
The patient reports feeling overly tired due to polypharmacy.
The doctor asks if the patient has had any cardiovascular testing.
The doctor asks if the patient is taking all prescribed medications.
The doctor asks to confirm family history of heart attack.
The patient clarifies her son had a myocardial infarction.
The patient reports her brother had a stroke and diabetes, and her sister has a heart problem.
The patient is a non-smoker and has no known drug allergies.
The doctor performs a physical exam including auscultation.
The doctor asks if the patient feels dizzy.
The doctor orders an echocardiogram to assess cardiac structure and function.
The doctor explains the suspicion of aortic stenosis.
The doctor discusses TAVR as a potential procedure if aortic stenosis is severe.
The doctor notes that surgical aortic valve replacement is not an option due to age and comorbidities.
The doctor advises continuing current amlodipine and lisinopril.
The doctor suggests discussing the potential discontinuation of simvastatin 20 mg with the PCP due to leg pain.
The doctor advises continuing levothyroxine.
The doctor schedules a follow-up in three to four weeks to discuss echocardiogram results.
